verbatim: It saves me at least an hour or two per contract.
I treat it like a brilliant intern who has read every document in the world but has absolutely zero real-world business context or common sense.
theme: Mental model of AI as junior associate

verbatim: They might give me an answer that sounds incredibly authoritative, but could be fundamentally missing the business context, or worse, misinterpreting a subtle linguistic nuance in

Absolutely. Just last month, we were reviewing an updated cross-border data privacy addendum. The AI assured me that the indemnification cap was reciprocal. When I actually read the clause, the grammar was structured with a double negative that effectively capped their liability while leaving our liability completely uncapped. The AI missed the syntactic inversion.
The number one thing I need is verifiability at the point of consumption. I absolutely despise tools that give me a clean, synthesized answer in a text box without inline citations.

If an AI legal assistant writes, 'Under French law, you have 30 days to cure this specific breach,' that text is completely useless to me unless there is a hyperlinked footnote right next to it that hooks into the exact section of the French Civil Code. And when I click that link, I don't want to be taken to a general homepage; I want a split-screen layout showing the exact paragraph of source text with the relevant words highlighted.
I need intuitive, structural building blocks—toggles, radio buttons, drop-down policies—where I can visually construct our legal posture without writing code or formatting perfect paragraphs.

Sales loves it because they want their contracts approved yesterday. They constantly pressure us, saying, 'Can’t you just run this through the AI and sign it?' They see AI as a magic 'Go Fast' button.
Our data cannot be used to train any public or third-party models. Period. Everything must live in a completely isolated, single-tenant cloud environment.
If that leaks into a shared model pool, it's game over.
I draw a hard line at autonomous execution or advice given directly to business units without a human lawyer in the loop.

A machine can look at a contract and say it violates Clause 4.B. But a human lawyer knows that this specific vendor is critical to a multi-million dollar launch happening next week, and that our risk appetite for this specific violation is actually quite high given the macro situation.
I would make them humble. The current UX paradigm of chat-based AI is built on the illusion of omniscience.
Give us control, give us transparency, and show us the receipts for every single claim the AI makes.
